chunks.text: #### 3.2 The Scalable Methods In this section, we categorize the scaling techniques based on the data sources, distinguishing between instruction and response generation, and further detail their respective construction methodologies. #### 3.2.1 Instruction Generation
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_020
chunks.text: #### 3.2.1 Instruction Generation Context-based methods augment LLMs by integrating external knowledge into inputs, enhancing the instructions' diversity and authenticity. However, they often incur costs and complexities in knowledge collection. For instance, Web-Instruct [\(Yue et al.,](#page-18-14) [2024\)](#page-18-14) retrieves and contextualizes documents from web databases to create an instruction dataset. At the same time, Backtranslation [\(Li et al.,](#page-13-11) [2024b\)](#page-13-11) utilizes 502K unlabeled text segments from the ClueWeb corpus to iteratively fine-tune a seed model and create high-quality training examples. Ditto [\(Lu et al.,](#page-14-13) [2024b\)](#page-14-13) employs a self-alignment method to simulate dialogues based on 4,000 high-quality role knowledge entries, and SOLID [\(Askari et al.,](#page-10-6) [2024\)](#page-10-6) automates prompt generation for dialogue through multi-intent instructions, amassing extensive data.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_021
chunks.text:
Evolution-based techniques mimic natural processes to refine instructions iteratively, thereby boosting diversity and quality through mutation prompts or self-instruction mechanisms. Evol-Instruct [\(Xu et al.,](#page-17-14) [2023\)](#page-17-14) uses deep and breadth evolution strategies to generate instructions of varied difficulty levels automatically. Promptbreeder [\(Fer](#page-11-8)[nando et al.,](#page-11-8) [2023\)](#page-11-8) employs an evolutionary algorithm with mutation prompts to improve task prompts via a binary tournament genetic process. DiverseEvol [\(Wu et al.,](#page-17-15) [2023a\)](#page-17-15) enhances data diversity by using a K-Center sampling algorithm

{4}------------------------------------------------

to select the most dissimilar data points iteratively. Moreover, Self-instruct [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-12) [2023c\)](#page-16-12) allows a model to autonomously generate and validate new task instructions from a seed dataset, gradually building a comprehensive pool of tasks.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_022
chunks.text: #### 3.2.2 Response Generation
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_023
chunks.text: #### 3.2.2 Response Generation Sampling strategies generate multiple candidate responses to a given instruction, selecting the highest-quality output using distinct algorithms. RFT [\(Yuan et al.,](#page-17-13) [2023a\)](#page-17-13) leverages a rejection sampling algorithm to filter reasoning paths, ensuring high-quality dataset generation. RAFT [\(Dong](#page-11-7) [et al.,](#page-11-7) [2023\)](#page-11-7) enhances model performance through reward-ranked fine-tuning - iteratively generating and selecting optimal responses. LMSI [\(Huang](#page-12-11) [et al.,](#page-12-11) [2022a\)](#page-12-11) refines predictions using Chain-of-Thought prompts and a majority voting mechanism, optimizing model fine-tuning. STaR [\(Zelikman](#page-18-12) [et al.,](#page-18-12) [2022\)](#page-18-12) and Quiet-STaR [\(Zelikman et al.,](#page-18-13) [2024\)](#page-18-13) further advance LLM reasoning by sampling rationales, with Quiet-STaR integrating parallel sampling for more diverse rationale generation.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_024
chunks.text: Self-Play is a significant avenue in model improvement, involving the model iterating against itself to refine strategies. This approach, rooted in game theory and illustrated by early checkers research [\(Samuel,](#page-15-11) [1959\)](#page-15-11), finds practical applications in various fields. SPIN [\(Chen et al.,](#page-11-6) [2024c\)](#page-11-6) implements self-play within LLMs to boost performance without labeled data. AMIE [\(Tu et al.,](#page-16-9) [2024\)](#page-16-9) uniquely applies self-play in the medical field, iterating internal and external self-play processes for accurate medical diagnostics. Self-Talk [\(Ulmer](#page-16-10) [et al.,](#page-16-10) [2024\)](#page-16-10) generates role-playing dialogue data via role simulations, further processed through automated quality filtering. Sotopia-π [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-11) [2024c\)](#page-16-11) uses GPT-4 [\(OpenAI,](#page-14-2) [2024a\)](#page-14-2) to create varied social scenarios and objectives, further extending social task generation.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_025
chunks.text:
Self-Refinement employs an iterative process where models refine their outputs through selffeedback, progressively enhancing response quality. SCORE [\(Zhang et al.,](#page-18-11) [2024f\)](#page-18-11) facilitates selfcorrection in small models using correct solutions as feedback prompts during generation. SELF [\(Lu](#page-14-12) [et al.\)](#page-14-12) introduces a meta-skill learning framework, enabling models to improve through selfevaluation of unlabelled instructions iteratively. SELF-ALIGN [\(Sun et al.,](#page-15-7) [2023\)](#page-15-7) ensures reliable response through continuous refinement based on

principles and demonstrations. ISR-LLM [\(Zhou](#page-19-1) [et al.,](#page-19-1) [2023d\)](#page-19-1) employs LLMs as self-verifiers, providing feedback for iterative plan refinement.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_026
chunks.text: Weak Supervision explores the potential of LLMs learning from outputs generated by weaker models. This approach addresses the growing disparity between model capabilities and human supervision limits. [\(Hase et al.,](#page-12-10) [2024\)](#page-12-10) illustrate LLMs' capacity to generalize from simple to complex tasks, validating weak supervision's feasibility. [\(Burns et al.,](#page-10-4) [2023\)](#page-10-4) focus on fine-tuning strong models using weak model outputs, introducing a performance gap recovered (PGR) metric for evaluating weak-to-strong generalization. [\(Bansal et al.,](#page-10-5) [2024\)](#page-10-5) challenge the superiority of strong but expensive models, demonstrating that weak but cheap models may offer higher computational efficiency.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_027
chunks.text: Takeaways 1 *We categorize scalable SFT methods into instruction and response approaches. For instructions, context-based methods enrich instructions with external knowledge but require reliable sources, while evolution-based methods automate instruction refinement but demand careful design. For responses, sampling achieves optimal distribution but may produce redundant outputs; self-play and self-refinement iteratively boost model performance through self-feedback but are limited by the model's initial capability. Weak supervision trains stronger models using outputs from weaker ones, enabling strong generalization but with the risk of propagating errors.* ### 4 Scaling for Reinforcement Learning ![](_page_4_Figure_9.jpeg) Figure 4: Classification of RL Methods. Reinforcement Learning (RL) refers to learning {5}------------------------------------------------
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_028
chunks.text: ![](_page_4_Figure_9.jpeg) Figure 4: Classification of RL Methods. Reinforcement Learning (RL) refers to learning {5}------------------------------------------------ from environments through interaction and rewards. Integrating RL with LLMs has become a promising area of research. Notably, InstructGPT [\(Ouyang](#page-14-10) [et al.,](#page-14-10) [2022\)](#page-14-10) introduces Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), enabling LLMs to understand human preferences via feedback, a technique foundational to ChatGPT [\(OpenAI,](#page-14-15) [2022\)](#page-14-15). Several studies [\(Yuan et al.,](#page-17-16) [2023d;](#page-17-16) [Dong et al.,](#page-11-7) [2023;](#page-11-7) [Lee et al.,](#page-13-9) [2024\)](#page-13-9) aim to enhance RLHF. Scaling RL is another critical focus, as learning from environment feedback is more complex but scalable than SFT. Facilitating LLMs' training through feedback is vital for advancing scaling laws. We classify existing practices combining RL and LLMs by feedback signals (Figure [4\)](#page-4-2) and analyze the scalability prospects of various RL for LLM algorithms.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_029
chunks.text: #### 4.1 The Common Methods Reinforcement Learning within LLMs traditionally serve as supplementary alignment techniques, such as in RLHF [\(Ouyang et al.,](#page-14-10) [2022;](#page-14-10) [Li et al.,](#page-13-12) [2023b;](#page-13-12) [Hu et al.,](#page-12-14) [2024\)](#page-12-14). Consequently, considerations of scalability are often overlooked. These approaches are usually implemented as incremental post-pretraining stages, using human labeling or reward modeling to fine-tune models' outputs. #### 4.1.1 Human Labeling
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_030
chunks.text: #### 4.1.1 Human Labeling Employing experts to provide direct alignment signals is intuitive but poses scalability challenges due to the time and cost required. Studies leverage human feedback or existing datasets for aligning LLMs, such as DPO [\(Rafailov et al.,](#page-15-6) [2024\)](#page-15-6), which optimizes reward maximization within a single policy phase, and KTO [\(Ethayarajh et al.,](#page-11-5) [2024\)](#page-11-5), which uses human-aware loss functions to eliminate the need for preference data. RRHF [\(Yuan](#page-17-12) [et al.,](#page-17-12) [2023c\)](#page-17-12) aligns model responses with human preferences through a ranking-based approach, and SimPO [\(Meng et al.,](#page-14-11) [2024\)](#page-14-11) enhances this by using length-normalized rewards and target reward differences, outperforming similar methods without additional reference models.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_031
chunks.text: ## 4.1.2 Reward Modeling The integration of model-based reward signals can significantly enhance the scalability of RLHF, yet still needs lots of human labeling. InstructGPT [\(Ouyang et al.,](#page-14-10) [2022\)](#page-14-10) integrates supervised policy training with human feedback, optimizing through Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) [\(Schulman et al.,](#page-15-12) [2017\)](#page-15-12). ReMax [\(Li](#page-13-10)
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_032
chunks.text: [et al.,](#page-13-10) [2024d\)](#page-13-10), leveraging the REINFORCE algorithm [\(Williams,](#page-17-17) [1987\)](#page-17-17), is more computationally efficient, and Fine-Grained Human Feedback [\(Wu](#page-17-11) [et al.,](#page-17-11) [2023b\)](#page-17-11) provides detailed feedback using several reward models. Both ORM and PRM [\(Cobbe](#page-11-1) [et al.,](#page-11-1) [2021;](#page-11-1) [Lightman et al.,](#page-13-3) [2023\)](#page-13-3) boost performance in mathematical tasks with human labeling. WARM [\(Ramé et al.,](#page-15-5) [2024\)](#page-15-5) mitigates reward hacking by fine-tuning and averaging multiple reward models. UNA [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-8) [2025\)](#page-16-8) unifies RLHF into a supervised learning problem through a generalized implicit reward function, reducing training instability and memory requirements.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_033
chunks.text: ### 4.2 The Scalable Methods As scaling pre-trained models hits its limits, LLM development increasingly focuses on boosting performance via Reinforcement Learning [\(Bai et al.,](#page-10-2) [2024;](#page-10-2) [Putta et al.,](#page-14-9) [2024;](#page-14-9) [Yuan et al.,](#page-17-8) [2024\)](#page-17-8). This involves automating feedback acquisition and improving alignment with human expectations. Scalable RL methods are categorized by the type of feedback signal: synthetic reward, environment, and self. #### 4.2.1 Synthetic Reward Modeling
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_034
chunks.text: #### 4.2.1 Synthetic Reward Modeling It emphasizes scalability and leverages synthetic data and iterative processes, thus reducing reliance on manual annotations for reward modeling. RLAIF [\(Bai et al.,](#page-10-3) [2022;](#page-10-3) [Lee et al.,](#page-13-9) [2024\)](#page-13-9) trains preference models with AI-generated feedback from constitutions, while RLSF [\(Kim et al.,](#page-12-9) [2023\)](#page-12-9) uses quality discrepancies among LLM responses to train a reward model. Q\* [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-7) [2024a\)](#page-16-7) optimizes state prioritization using historical and future rewards, and IterAlign [\(Chen et al.,](#page-11-3) [2024b\)](#page-11-3) employs a red team approach for selfalignment and constitution discovery. Additionally, [\(Sun et al.,](#page-15-4) [2024\)](#page-15-4) implements easy-to-hard generator generalization through evaluators trained under easy task supervision.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_035
chunks.text: ### 4.2.2 Environment Feedback It is a key reward signal for training LLMs as agents in various settings from real-world simulations [\(Shridhar et al.,](#page-15-13) [2021\)](#page-15-13) to digital environments [\(Zhou et al.,](#page-18-17) [2024a;](#page-18-17) [Rawles et al.,](#page-15-14) [2024a\)](#page-15-14) and rule-based systems [\(Côté et al.,](#page-11-11) [2019\)](#page-11-11). This feedback reduces the need for manually labeled data and provides consistent, reliable signals if simulations closely mimic real-world scenarios. Notable contributions include DigiRL [\(Bai et al.,](#page-10-2) {6}------------------------------------------------
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_036
chunks.text: {6}------------------------------------------------ [2024\)](#page-10-2), creating a parallel GUI learning environment, and ENVISIONS [\(Xu et al.,](#page-17-10) [2024a\)](#page-17-10), featuring LLM-generated trajectories interacting with simulations. Additionally, SANDBOX [\(Liu et al.,](#page-13-7) [2023b\)](#page-13-7) and RLTF [\(Liu et al.,](#page-13-8) [2023a\)](#page-13-8) explore interactive feedback and unit tests, while languagerule environments employ innovative methods like SPAG [\(Cheng et al.,](#page-11-2) [2024b\)](#page-11-2) and Prover-Verifier Games [\(Kirchner et al.,](#page-12-8) [2024\)](#page-12-8) for training models through adversarial and verification tasks. ### 4.2.3 Self-Feedback
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_037
chunks.text: ### 4.2.3 Self-Feedback It evaluates policy trajectories to enhance the generation capabilities of LLMs using the generationdiscrimination gap. Techniques like RLCD [\(Yang](#page-17-7) [et al.,](#page-17-7) [2024c\)](#page-17-7), Agent Q [\(Putta et al.,](#page-14-9) [2024\)](#page-14-9), and Self-Rewarding [\(Yuan et al.,](#page-17-8) [2024\)](#page-17-8) use comparative outputs, guided MCTS, and self-evaluation. The Meta-Rewarding framework [\(Wu et al.,](#page-17-9) [2024a\)](#page-17-9) has models that assess their evaluations to improve both generative and evaluative abilities. The Self-Taught Evaluator [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-6) [2024d\)](#page-16-6) refines models by iteratively modifying input prompts, achieving superior performance on benchmarks like Reward-Bench [\(Lambert et al.,](#page-13-13) [2024b\)](#page-13-13).
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_038
chunks.text: Takeaways 2 *We categorize scalable RL methods based on the source of reward signals: Synthetic Reward Modeling constructs signals through rules, but it may have biases; Environment Feedback involves extensive interactions and feedback within virtual environments, but the construction cost is high, and it may not align with real-world scenarios; Self-Feedback uses self-evaluation as a signal, making it the easiest to scale, but the model's capabilities limit it.* ### 5 Scaling for Test-time Compute ![](_page_6_Figure_5.jpeg) Figure 5: Classification of TTC Methods. Test-time Compute refers to a model's inference phase, predicting outputs like the next word in a sentence. Scaling Test-time Compute, introduced by OpenAI's o1 [\(OpenAI,](#page-14-3) [2024b\)](#page-14-3), can further enhance model performance in various scenarios. This section explores common and scalable methods for improving model performance by increasing inference computation (Figure [5\)](#page-6-6). ## 5.1 The Common Methods
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_039
chunks.text: ## 5.1 The Common Methods Prior research aims to enhance model performance by incorporating additional computational processes during inference. However, many of them do not consider scalability. The common methods can be divided into two categories: In-Context Learning (ICL) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT). #### 5.1.1 In-Context Learning
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_040
chunks.text: #### 5.1.1 In-Context Learning Exemplified by GPT-3 [\(Brown,](#page-10-0) [2020\)](#page-10-0), ICL enables LLMs to adapt to specific tasks without parameter updates by providing predefined context. Early manual prompt engineering approaches [\(Petroni](#page-14-16) [et al.,](#page-14-16) [2019;](#page-14-16) [Schick and Schütze,](#page-15-15) [2020\)](#page-15-15) are not scalable or automated. Recent advancements, such as automated prompt learning methods like instruction induction [\(Honovich et al.,](#page-12-7) [2022b\)](#page-12-7), APE [\(Zhou](#page-19-0) [et al.,](#page-19-0) [2022\)](#page-19-0), and OPRO [\(Yang et al.,](#page-17-6) [2024b\)](#page-17-6), address some of these issues but still face challenges in performance scaling with increased input tokens during inference.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_041
chunks.text: ## 5.1.2 Chain-of-Thought
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_042
chunks.text: ## 5.1.2 Chain-of-Thought CoT [\(Wei et al.,](#page-16-5) [2023a\)](#page-16-5) enables LLMs to reason, enhances performance in logic and calculations, and offers greater flexibility and scalability. Research in CoT includes using prompting [\(Kojima](#page-12-5) [et al.,](#page-12-5) [2023;](#page-12-5) [Zhou et al.,](#page-18-10) [2023b;](#page-18-10) [Khot et al.,](#page-12-15) [2022\)](#page-12-15) and training [\(Lewkowycz et al.,](#page-13-6) [2022\)](#page-13-6) to stimulate reasoning capabilities of LLMs. CoT can also integrate with tools to enhance performance in domains like mathematics and coding [\(Gao](#page-12-16) [et al.,](#page-12-16) [2023;](#page-12-16) [Chen et al.,](#page-11-12) [2023a\)](#page-11-12) through approaches like ReAct [\(Yao et al.,](#page-17-5) [2022b\)](#page-17-5) and Inner Monologue [\(Huang et al.,](#page-12-6) [2022b\)](#page-12-6).
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_043
chunks.text: #### 5.2 The Scalable Methods In the post-GPT era, as model sizes grow, increasing parameters becomes challenging. Thus, attention shifts to scaling TTC. [\(Snell et al.,](#page-15-1) [2024\)](#page-15-1) explores inference-time computation scalability in LLMs, proposing a "compute-optimal" scaling strategy. [\(Li et al.,](#page-13-14) [2024c\)](#page-13-14) shows that TTC can {7}------------------------------------------------ significantly enhance models' expressive capabilities, demonstrating the feasibility of addressing larger-scale problems with adequate computational resources. This section categorizes various methods for augmenting computation. #### 5.2.1 Sampling
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_044
chunks.text: #### 5.2.1 Sampling Sampling is a fundamental technique to boost performance by increasing test-time computation. Given the inherently stochastic nature of contemporary LLM generation strategies [\(Chen et al.,](#page-10-10) [2021a;](#page-10-10) [Holtzman et al.,](#page-12-17) [2020;](#page-12-17) [Zhu et al.,](#page-19-2) [2023\)](#page-19-2), selecting the correct answer from multiple samples for a single query enhances performance, with improvements scaling with the number of samples [\(Chen](#page-10-11) [et al.,](#page-10-11) [2021b;](#page-10-11) [Wang et al.,](#page-16-4) [2023a\)](#page-16-4). Early approaches like Self-Consistency [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-4) [2023a\)](#page-16-4) improve performance in mathematical domains by generating multiple reasoning paths and selecting the majority answer. Verification-guided weighted majority voting further enhances models like GPT-4 in solving mathematical problems [\(Zhou et al.,](#page-18-9) [2023a\)](#page-18-9). Additionally, [\(Liu et al.,](#page-13-5) [2023c;](#page-13-5) [Nakano](#page-14-8) [et al.,](#page-14-8) [2022;](#page-14-8) [Cobbe et al.,](#page-11-1) [2021\)](#page-11-1) employs reward models to select human-aligned responses and solve mathematical problems.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_045
chunks.text: #### 5.2.2 Verified Chain-of-Thought
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_046
chunks.text: #### 5.2.2 Verified Chain-of-Thought Verifying reasoning paths is critical as the correctness of each inferential step significantly impacts LLM performance [\(Weng et al.,](#page-17-3) [2023\)](#page-17-3). Various methods propose enhancing the reliability and scalability of CoT reasoning. PRM [\(Lightman et al.,](#page-13-3) [2023\)](#page-13-3) verify each reasoning step, outperforming majority voting. SelfCheck [\(Miao et al.,](#page-14-7) [2023\)](#page-14-7) allows for step verification to identify and regenerate erroneous steps, improving long-chain reliability. DiVeRSe [\(Li et al.,](#page-13-4) [2023a\)](#page-13-4) uses diverse prompts and weighted voting to enhance performance. Logi-CoT [\(Zhao et al.,](#page-18-8) [2024b\)](#page-18-8) integrates symbolic logic to verify and adjust reasoning processes, mitigating hallucinations due to logical errors.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_047
chunks.text: ### 5.2.3 Searching
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_048
chunks.text: ### 5.2.3 Searching Searching utilizes different reasoning structures to explore in space. Tree-of-Thought (ToT) [\(Yao et al.,](#page-17-4) [2023\)](#page-17-4) self-evaluates and backtracks multiple reasoning paths, enhancing problem-solving. Diverse search algorithms [\(Golovneva et al.,](#page-12-4) [2023;](#page-12-4) [Zhang](#page-18-5) [et al.,](#page-18-5) [2023b,](#page-18-5) [2024e;](#page-18-6) [Sel et al.,](#page-15-3) [2024\)](#page-15-3) like Pathfinder [\(Golovneva et al.,](#page-12-4) [2023\)](#page-12-4), Cumulative Reasoning [\(Zhang et al.,](#page-18-5) [2023b\)](#page-18-5), Graph of Thoughts [\(Besta](#page-10-1)

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_049
chunks.text: [et al.,](#page-10-1) [2024\)](#page-10-1), and Diagram of Thought [\(Zhang et al.,](#page-18-6) [2024e\)](#page-18-6) optimize tree searches, iterative methods, graph models, and directed acyclic graphs, respectively. Algorithm of Thoughts [\(Sel et al.,](#page-15-3) [2024\)](#page-15-3) further optimizes ToT algorithms for computational efficiency, and MCTS explores boosting test-time performance [\(Liu et al.,](#page-13-15) [2024a;](#page-13-15) [Feng et al.,](#page-11-13) [2024;](#page-11-13) [Tian et al.,](#page-16-15) [2024;](#page-16-15) [Zhang et al.,](#page-18-7) [2024b\)](#page-18-7). ### 5.2.4 Long In-Context Learning
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_050
chunks.text: ### 5.2.4 Long In-Context Learning With the ongoing research in automated ICL, performance improvements become unstable as the number of examples increases, thereby underutilizing the potential of multi-instance contextual learning [\(Lu et al.,](#page-14-6) [2022\)](#page-14-6). Consequently, Many-Shot ICL [\(Agarwal et al.,](#page-9-0) [2024\)](#page-9-0) devises an automated example construction technique, extending examples to up to 2048 shots, leading to noticeable advancements across various domains. Another approach [\(Hsieh et al.,](#page-12-3) [2023\)](#page-12-3) combines a greedy algorithm with beam search to optimize the method for long prompts, thereby enhancing the scaling performance of long in-context learning. #### 5.2.5 Self-Verification
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_051
chunks.text: #### 5.2.5 Self-Verification Enabling LLMs to self-verify at test time improves performance by bridging the gap between generation and discrimination [\(Ng and Jordan,](#page-14-17) [2001;](#page-14-17) [Zheng et al.,](#page-18-18) [2023\)](#page-18-18). Self-Refine [\(Press et al.,](#page-14-5) [2022\)](#page-14-5) uses iterative self-feedback to enhance outputs, while Reflexion [\(Shinn et al.,](#page-15-2) [2024\)](#page-15-2) mitigates hallucinations and inefficiencies through environmental feedback. [\(Li et al.,](#page-13-2) [2024a\)](#page-13-2) propose a self-checking paradigm for comparing candidate answers, and Self-Verification [\(Weng et al.,](#page-17-3) [2023\)](#page-17-3) provides an explainable validation score via reverse verification. Self-Contrast [\(Zhang et al.,](#page-18-4) [2024d\)](#page-18-4) generates and compares different solutions to avoid biases and inconsistencies during re-evaluation.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_052
chunks.text: Takeaways 3 *We categorize scalable TTC methods as follows: Sampling is the simplest scaling method but may produce redundant similar output. Verified CoT aims to address the scalability issue limited by error propagation in CoT. Searching aims to explore optimal paths in the reasoning space, utilizing various reasoning structures. Long ICL enhances model output through many unsupervised examples. Self-verification improves performance through self-correction. These meth-* {8}------------------------------------------------ *ods exchange model performance with inference computation.* ### 6 Potential Applications As LLMs continue improving, research has shifted from chat-based applications to productivity-based ones. Post-training efforts now focus on enhancing these productivity applications. This section discusses these emerging, promising applications. ## 6.1 Mathematics

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_053
chunks.text:
As LLMs continue improving, research has shifted from chat-based applications to productivity-based ones. Post-training efforts now focus on enhancing these productivity applications. This section discusses these emerging, promising applications.

## 6.1 Mathematics

LLMs have long struggled with Mathematics, but recent advancements are changing this landscape. Efforts to scale training data include using extensive datasets from the web [\(Lewkowycz et al.,](#page-13-6) [2022;](#page-13-6) [Taylor et al.,](#page-16-16) [2022;](#page-16-16) [Yue et al.,](#page-18-14) [2024;](#page-18-14) [Tosh](#page-16-17)[niwal et al.,](#page-16-17) [2024a\)](#page-16-17). Some works focus on generating CoT training data [\(Zelikman et al.,](#page-18-12) [2022;](#page-18-12) [Wang et al.,](#page-16-18) [2024b;](#page-16-18) [Toshniwal et al.,](#page-16-19) [2024b;](#page-16-19) [Qiao](#page-15-16) [et al.,](#page-15-16) [2024\)](#page-15-16) and using RMs to select high-quality data [\(Luo et al.,](#page-14-18) [2023;](#page-14-18) [Xu et al.,](#page-17-18) [2024b;](#page-17-18) [Yang](#page-17-19) [et al.,](#page-17-19) [2024a;](#page-17-19) [Cobbe et al.,](#page-11-1) [2021\)](#page-11-1). Other works enhance mathematical reasoning using self-generated data. For instance, LMSI [\(Huang et al.,](#page-12-11) [2022a\)](#page-12-11) uses high-confidence responses, while RFT [\(Yuan](#page-17-20) [et al.,](#page-17-20) [2023b\)](#page-17-20) applies correct answers for filtering. RL also strengthens LLMs in math. PRM [\(Light](#page-13-3)[man et al.,](#page-13-3) [2023\)](#page-13-3) introduces step supervision, and DeepSeek-Math improves PPO efficiency [\(Shao](#page-15-17) [et al.,](#page-15-17) [2024\)](#page-15-17). Math-Shepherd [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-18) [2024b\)](#page-16-18) implements step-PPO via PRM, whereas Step-DPO [\(Lai et al.,](#page-13-16) [2024b\)](#page-13-16) focuses on individual reasoning steps for DPO. Lastly, OmegaPRM [\(Luo](#page-14-19) [et al.,](#page-14-19) [2024b\)](#page-14-19) uses a divide-and-conquer style MCTS to gather high-quality supervision data.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_054
chunks.text: #### 6.2 Code Generation
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_055
chunks.text:
#### 6.2 Code Generation

Despite the advancements in LLMs' code generation abilities, they still struggle with complex engineering problems [\(Chen et al.,](#page-11-14) [2021c;](#page-11-14) [Li et al.,](#page-13-17) [2022;](#page-13-17) [Fried et al.,](#page-12-18) [2022;](#page-12-18) [Lai et al.,](#page-13-18) [2023;](#page-13-18) [Nijkamp](#page-14-20) [et al.,](#page-14-20) [2022\)](#page-14-20). Scalable post-training techniques are needed, with a key aspect being feedback from the runtime environment for optimization. One approach involves using external tools like code interpreters, as seen in LDB [\(Zhong et al.,](#page-18-19) [2024\)](#page-18-19) and SelfEvolve [\(Jiang et al.,](#page-12-19) [2023\)](#page-12-19), which help LLMs execute code and obtain error feedback. SelfDebugging [\(Chen et al.,](#page-11-15) [2023b\)](#page-11-15) focuses on optimizing code through feedback from execution outcomes. For repository-level code, RepoCoder [\(Zhang et al.,](#page-18-20)
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_056
chunks.text:
[2023a\)](#page-18-20) uses a retrieval framework to extract valuable information from repositories during generation. ARKS [\(Su et al.,](#page-15-18) [2024\)](#page-15-18) creates a "knowledge soup" to improve code generation iteratively. RLEF [\(Gehring et al.,](#page-12-20) [2024\)](#page-12-20) proposes an RL approach to enhance LLMs in code synthesis by utilizing execution feedback to improve code iteratively.

#### 6.3 Agent Execution
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_057
chunks.text:
#### 6.3 Agent Execution

The growing capabilities of LLMs extend beyond text generation and program synthesis to include device control in simulated environments like web and android platforms [\(Bai et al.,](#page-10-2) [2024;](#page-10-2) [Lai et al.,](#page-12-21) [2024a;](#page-12-21) [Zhou et al.,](#page-18-21) [2023c;](#page-18-21) [Zhang et al.,](#page-18-22) [2024c;](#page-18-22) [Xu](#page-17-21) [et al.,](#page-17-21) [2024c;](#page-17-21) [Rawles et al.,](#page-15-19) [2024b;](#page-15-19) [Deng et al.,](#page-11-16) [2024;](#page-11-16) [Yao et al.,](#page-17-22) [2022a\)](#page-17-22). Research involves searching and RL strategies, which generate trajectories through online interaction and are evaluated by reward models [\(Pan et al.,](#page-14-21) [2024;](#page-14-21) [Qi et al.,](#page-15-20) [2024;](#page-15-20) [Liu](#page-13-19) [et al.,](#page-13-19) [2024b\)](#page-13-19). DigiRL [\(Bai et al.,](#page-10-2) [2024\)](#page-10-2) employs online learning and AWR for policy updates, excelling on the AITW benchmark [\(Rawles et al.,](#page-15-19) [2024b\)](#page-15-19). Archer [\(Zhou et al.,](#page-18-23) [2024b\)](#page-18-23) uses hierarchical RL for multi-round decision-making, while AgentQ [\(Putta et al.,](#page-14-9) [2024\)](#page-14-9) leverages MCTS and DPO for searching and policy updates [\(Rafailov](#page-15-6) [et al.,](#page-15-6) [2024\)](#page-15-6).
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_058
chunks.text: # 7 Conclusion The landscape of LLM training is evolving as traditional pre-training scaling shows limitations. This survey examines the new paradigm of post-training scaling, specifically SFT, RLxF, and TTC. By comparing these methods and highlighting their potential to overcome computational and data limitations, we provide insights for future research, setting the stage for integrating them into more efficient and sustainable advancements in LLMs. ### Acknowledgement This work has been supported by the NSFC (624B2084), NSFC for Distinguished Young Scholar (62425601) and New Cornerstone Science Foundation through the XPLORER PRIZE. {9}------------------------------------------------ ## Limitations This section discusses several challenges and future directions of the post-training scaling in LLMs.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_059
chunks.text:
{9}------------------------------------------------

## Limitations

This section discusses several challenges and future directions of the post-training scaling in LLMs.

Theoretical Foundation. Existing methods for scaling post-training rely heavily on empirical experience and need a theoretical foundation. Some research efforts focus on theoretical analysis. [\(Li](#page-13-14) [et al.,](#page-13-14) [2024c\)](#page-13-14) provided theoretical evidence for TTC by demonstrating how it enhances the sequential computation capability of Transformer architectures, addressing their inherent limitations in handling serial reasoning tasks. [\(Snell et al.,](#page-15-1) [2024\)](#page-15-1) examined the theoretical support of selfimprovement mechanisms, explaining how the revision-and-refine approach modifies implicit input distributions to enhance model reasoning. [\(Ngo](#page-14-22) [et al.,](#page-14-22) [2024\)](#page-14-22) developed a theoretical framework for analyzing alignment problems, clarifying how RLHF mitigates distribution shifts to improve performance. Additionally, [\(Dai et al.,](#page-11-17) [2023\)](#page-11-17) provided theoretical insights into instruction tuning from a gradient descent perspective, explaining how it implicitly optimizes models to acquire in-context learning abilities.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_060
chunks.text:
Synthetic Data. To support the scaling of posttraining, constructing high-quality synthetic data is essential. Although synthetic data have shown considerable success in advancing model abilities, several studies have highlighted the dark side of synthetic data, including model collapse [\(Shumailov](#page-15-0) [et al.,](#page-15-0) [2024\)](#page-15-0). Furthermore, ensuring data diversity presents a significant challenge. Without human intervention, augmented data often do not exceed the distribution of the initial seed set. Thus, synthesizing high-quality data while mitigating potential risks remains an open problem.

Continual Learning. The ideal approach for post-training involves continuously collecting data from dynamic environments, such as the real world, to incrementally enhance LLM's performance. The primary objective is to enable the model to acquire new skills and knowledge over time while preserving its existing capabilities. In the context of scaling post-training for LLMs, addressing the alignment tax [\(Ouyang et al.,](#page-14-10) [2022\)](#page-14-10) and mitigating the forgetting of acquired knowledge is crucial.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_061
chunks.text:
Active Exploration. Current post-training methods rely on manually curated or model-augmented data derived from a human-collected seed set. This

can introduce biases due to intrinsic human knowledge limitations, and it remains unclear whether these human-inspected datasets are practical for scaling. Another approach involves empowering the model to identify underperforming areas and actively generate targeted data for enhancement. Recent studies have explored the utilization of advanced LLMs as teachers, dynamically assessing the performance of a student model and generating specific training samples accordingly [\(Cheng](#page-11-18) [et al.,](#page-11-18) [2024a;](#page-11-18) [Lu et al.,](#page-14-23) [2024a\)](#page-14-23). However, challenges arise in self-evolution scenarios. For example, self-evaluation bias can present significant difficulties, as an accurate assessment of the model's performance is crucial before generating supplementary training data. Future research should focus on enabling models to actively self-discover effective training samples, thereby facilitating their autonomous learning processes.
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_062
chunks.text: Superalignment. The base model could become incredibly powerful as we scale the model size and data. However, the methodologies to effectively conduct post-training to fully harness these capabilities still need to be explored, also known as the weak-to-strong generalization problem [\(Burns](#page-10-4) [et al.,](#page-10-4) [2023\)](#page-10-4). Moreover, significant efforts must be dedicated to safety post-training to ensure these models do not pose any risks. It is crucial to discern whether a model is genuinely safe or merely simulating safe behavior [\(Wang et al.,](#page-16-20) [2023b\)](#page-16-20).
chunks.paper_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models

chunks.chunk_id: 2025.acl-long.140_A Survey of Post-Training Scaling in Large Language Models_chunk_063
chunks.text:
Evaluation Metrics and Benchmarks. The evaluation of post-training effectiveness has traditionally depended on static benchmarks. However, with the increasing capabilities of LLMs, it is imperative to design more challenging and comprehensive benchmarks. Additionally, issues such as data leakage [\(Yang et al.,](#page-17-23) [2023;](#page-17-23) [Wei et al.,](#page-16-21) [2023b\)](#page-16-21) and leaderboard saturation [\(Guo et al.,](#page-12-22) [2023\)](#page-12-22) have become prevalent. To address these challenges, it is essential to innovate new evaluation methods, such as dynamic and automated leaderboards, and to develop novel metrics that effectively assess the impact of scaling.

(2024) The paper proposes verifier engineering, a novel post-training approach for enhancing LLMs. It uses
automated verifiers to perform verification and provide feedback. The process is divided into three stages:
search, verify, and feedback. |

both general and domain-specific data. | | Qu et al. (2024) | Recursive IntroSpEction (RISE) is a method for fine-tuning LLMs to enable them to iteratively improve

an iterative procedure, treating fine-tuning for a single-turn prompt as solving a multi-turn Markov decision
process. | | | Zhao et al. (2024a) This paper explores using synthetic data to fine-tune LLMs' handling of retrieval and reasoning in long
context tasks. The research varies the realism of key "needle" concepts and the diversity of surrounding
"haystack" contexts, comparing models trained on synthetic data versus real data. |
